---
title: "2.10 Implementing String Algorithms"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## String Processing

### Loops can traverse strings character by character

This topic combines:

::: {.tight-list}
- string indexing
- substring methods
- conditionals
- loops
:::

The goal is to recognize standard <span class="term">string-processing algorithms</span>.

## String Indices and Useful Methods

### Most string loops start at index 0

![String indices](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-10-implementing-string-algorithms/stringIndicies.png){fig-align="center" width="58%"}

The core methods reused in this topic are:

::: {.tight-list}
- `length()`
- `indexOf(...)`
- `substring(from, to)`
- `substring(from)`
:::

## while Find and Replace

### Use `indexOf` to keep searching until the target is gone

```java
while (s.indexOf("a") >= 0)
{
    index = s.indexOf("a");
    s = s.substring(0, index) + s.substring(index + 1);
}
```

This pattern works when:

::: {.tight-list}
- you do not know how many matches there will be
- each match is processed and removed or replaced
- the string changes after each pass
:::

## Replacement Algorithms

### A loop can repeatedly clean or rewrite text

```java
String firstpart = message.substring(0, index);
String lastpart = message.substring(index + 1);
message = firstpart + "l" + lastpart;
```

The important teaching move is not the exact example.  
It is the algorithm:

::: {.tight-list}
- find the target
- split the string around it
- rebuild a new string
- repeat until no target remains
:::

## `for` Loops over Strings

### Use `for` when you intend to visit every character

```java
for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++)
{
    String ithLetter = s.substring(i, i + 1);
}
```

The standard loop shape is:

::: {.tight-list}
- start at 0
- continue while `i < s.length()`
- process the character at `i`
:::

## Reversing a String

### Build a new string one character at a time

```java
String sReversed = "";
for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++)
{
    String ithLetter = s.substring(i, i + 1);
    sReversed = ithLetter + sReversed;
}
```

This is a good example of:

::: {.tight-list}
- traversal
- building a result gradually
- order mattering in concatenation
:::

## Classroom Task

### String Replacement: Cats and Dogs

![Cats and dogs challenge](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-10-implementing-string-algorithms/catordog.jpg){fig-align="center" width="28%"}

Retained task focus:

::: {.tight-list}
- find every occurrence of a target substring
- replace it with a new substring
- trace repeated string rebuilding
- <span class="term">2.10.3 Coding Challenge: String Replacement Cats and Dogs</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain when string algorithms use `while` versus `for`
- apply `indexOf` and `substring` to locate and rebuild strings
- use correct index bounds when traversing a string
- explain how a reversed string is built character by character
- recognize string-processing loops as repeated search-and-update patterns
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
